summary: Graham argues that experienced programmers develop a “hacker’s radar” that can often judge technologies by their external signals, and he applies that radar to Java. He lists cues—heavy hype, committee design, bureaucratic culture, corporate motives, and who adopts it—that make Java look suspicious, while admitting he hasn’t used it and could be wrong.
key_points: He frames the essay as a study of “hacker’s radar,” not a technical critique of Java itself.
Technologies that become real standards usually don’t need aggressive promotion; hype is a warning sign.
Languages designed to please large organizations or committees tend to be worse than those built by and for their creators.
Who loves a language (or doesn’t) and who is forced to use it are strong signals about its quality.
Programmers must triage what to learn, so dismissing some technologies by external signs is unavoidable.
money_quotes: Over time, hackers develop a nose for good (and bad) technology.
Real standards don't have to be promoted.
The good languages have been those that were designed for their own creators: C, Perl, Smalltalk, Lisp.
Hackers don't like a language that talks down to them. Hackers just want power.
There are too many technologies out there to learn them all. You have to learn to judge by outward signs which will be worth your time.
key_insight: When time is limited, seasoned programmers use external signals—hype, motives, design culture, and who adopts a tool—to decide whether a technology is worth learning.
